speaker: interviewee
text: She's Indian. And she was in Vienna now-- Vienna, Budapest, Prague-- little excursion. She said, if she-- if looks could kill, she would have been killed because she's black in Vienna. She said it was impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, perhaps you can add that to what you're saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, I don't want to. No. I'll just tell you. They were the good Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it's indicative of-- of the same feelings that created what happened in Europe still being there. I think it's important.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know what to say. I can't put it in good--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's an anecdote. You could just give it as she said it. But should we do the-- I'm sorry. This is tape six. It's October 31, 1996. I'm talking to Eva Schutz. The location is London, England. Can we screen that out? Can I do it again?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape seven. The date is the 31 of October, 1996. I am talking to Eva Schutz. The location is London, England.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to say after all I went through. I know it was very difficult sometimes. It was more than one thought you can stand. You had to be very-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you had to have chutzpah to get through all that. And I was all the time thinking of my mother, who taught me how to cope with small things, with big things, with difficult things, and just have an aim and get there, whatever it costs. Well, I don't think that I paid very much, just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm a bit bitter. I even don't want to forget sometimes. But I certainly won't forgive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what you'd like to leave as a message?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't think it's a good message.

speaker: interviewee
text: We can stop now, then go over to the-- --remember the name of his-- the person who took him under his wing when he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, he was-- I'll tell you who it was. It was Sir something.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was his-- they called it his department of gastroenterology. That was his unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry about this. No, that's fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us something about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family tree, showing all my grandfathers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you could say just very quickly-- [INAUDIBLE]. He died in Argentine of peritonitis.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't mention when he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't mention when he died, I don't think. If you want to-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] If you want to, you can say he died in the Argentine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me see. He was in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So that'll give you the opportunity to to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me the name of this woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel Bernstein. It was taken for a passport, I suppose, in 1932 or '33.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where would it have been taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, George Bernstein, who, after finishing the [NON-ENGLISH] Academy left for Paris, had a job there. But this photograph-- this photograph is taken in Krinitza, when he came to say goodbye to me before he went to Argentine. He died in Argentine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when the photograph was taken? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband. The photograph was taken in England. I don't know the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us your husband's name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know where. It could have been Scotland. It could have been Shropshire. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Wisza Stelmachowska and [NON-ENGLISH], her sister, the daughters of Mrs. Stelmachowska, who helped me so much during the war. The photograph was taken when I was in Warsaw. I can't remember the date. It must be the late '70s

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this photograph ?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Jan Schutz, my son. The photograph was taken in Canada, no idea the-- when-- which year. It must have been 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we do that one again? Unfortunately, what happened there-- with your elbow on the table, you wobbled the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, again? This is my son, Jan Schutz. This photograph was taken in Canada. I don't know which year. It's one of those summer photographs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. I thought that, but then I thought no. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell us about this document, Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my false-- it is my baptist. Yes, that's my false document of the birth-- my birth certificate. False, in the name of Sarnecka, born. [NON-ENGLISH], completely false.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was done in year '41 or '42. I don't know. I don't know when they did it. I got it in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [INAUDIBLE]. There's a stamp, and then there's a printed 1943. Are you ready? Can you tell us about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Jan's school [INAUDIBLE], which he got when he went to this school for children of servicemen. It was a boarding school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing more to say about it. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's do a close-up of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, yes, because it's the only one that shows the woman that did all these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this document and the photograph?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, sorry. Will you tell me about this document and the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a kind of identity card-- certificate, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] given to me when I was in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then it was given to me in '46 before I left Italy, yes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And there's also the date of Jan's birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And that's in your book as well?

speaker: interviewer
text: Should we just have a closeup of the photos?

speaker: interviewer
text: You want a photo of the photograph?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah. If you-- we have a connection with the Jewish community in the Ukraine-- helping them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you in touch with Dr. Weiss?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they do a lot for [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: The visa to allow me to come to England in '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell us about this piece of paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the permit to travel to Ludwigsburg, where I was supposed to work from when they took me from Kielce. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
